text: Rapidly doubling training volume over 4 weeks (from ~86 km/week to ~175 km/week) induced overtraining symptoms in experienced middle- and long-distance runners, with 6 of 8 showing decreased endurance performance and 2 showing stagnation.
Overtrained runners showed decreased maximum working capacity, with total treadmill running distance dropping from 4719m to 4361m during incremental testing.

Runners who developed overtraining could neither improve nor approximately reach their personal records during the subsequent competitive season, indicating prolonged performance impairment beyond the overload period.
Subjective complaint scores increased from 1.2 to 3.2 on a four-point scale during the overtraining period, indicating that athlete self-reported wellness tracks overtraining development.
Overtraining caused significant decreases in resting and exercise heart rate, which can serve as a physiological marker of parasympathetic overtraining syndrome.
Running velocity at the aerobic-anaerobic transition (lactate threshold) stagnated or declined during overtraining, making threshold pace a useful diagnostic marker.
Blood lactate levels decreased significantly during overtraining, consistent with glycogen depletion and reduced sympathetic drive rather than improved fitness.
